title: About
text: A standing read of the three monthly U.S. labour-market releases — Establishment Survey (CES), Household Survey (CPS), and JOLTS — that updates each release with the same set of charts, tables, and analytical framings. The aim is to put the same headline numbers, the same sectoral breakdowns, the same alternative measures, and the same diffusion / Sahm / V-U / quits-by-industry views in front of you on jobs-Friday morning. Year to year and across cycles you can compare the same picture.

title: About
text: A standing read of the three monthly U.S. labour-market releases — Establishment Survey (CES), Household Survey (CPS), and JOLTS — that updates each release with the same set of charts, tables, and analytical framings. The aim is to put the same headline numbers, the same sectoral breakdowns, the same alternative measures, and the same diffusion / Sahm / V-U / quits-by-industry views in front of you on jobs-Friday morning. Year to year and across cycles you can compare the same picture.

title: About
text:
Data source
All series are pulled from Haver Analytics. There is no FRED dependency. Each rebuild fetches every series in a single round-trip and caches the result on disk; if Haver is unreachable, the dashboard rebuilds from the cached CSV.

Last data refresh: 2026-04-29T15:26:07Z
Series in catalogue: 127
Bundle range: 2000-01-01 → 2026-03-01
Bundle dimensions: 315 rows × 819 columns

title: About
text:
Shutdown gap and interpolation
The September–November 2025 federal-government shutdown caused the BLS to miss the October 2025 release of the Household Survey (CPS). The Establishment Survey (CES) and JOLTS were not interrupted.
We searched Haver for shutdown-imputed variants of the affected codes (LR, LE, LF, LP, LQ, MLU5, MLU6, FA53, the unemployment-by-duration series, etc.) and none are published — the standard codes carry NaN for the missed month. The build pipeline therefore applies pandas time-based linear interpolation across interior gaps only, never extrapolating leading or trailing NaN. The interpolated month is logged in bundle_meta.json.

Series interpolated this build: 57.
Months filled: 2025-10-01, 2025-11-01.

title: About
text:
Revision policy
CES estimates are revised in the two months following first release and again at the annual benchmark each February. CPS is revised much less often. JOLTS levels are revised regularly — particularly the most recent two months. This dashboard always shows the latest vintage; older vintages are not preserved.

title: About
text:
Contact
Questions / corrections: see the linked GitHub repo footer.

Source: Haver Analytics. Joshua Bailey

title: Wages
text:
Average hourly earnings (AHE) is the establishment-survey wage tracker. It is not quality-adjusted — composition shifts can move it independently of any underlying wage pressure — but it is timely. The 3-month annualised view captures recent momentum; the year-on-year view smooths through monthly noise.










Figure 1: AHE growth — toggle between 3m, 6m annualised, and YoY.














Figure 2: All employees vs production & nonsupervisory — the latter strips out high-end compositional shifts.














Figure 3





Source: Haver Analytics. Joshua Bailey

title: Establishment Survey
text: The establishment (CES) survey of payroll employment was not interrupted by the 2025 federal-government shutdown — every series here has continuous monthly data through the latest release. Toggles let you switch between monthly, 3-month, and 6-month moving averages.

title: Establishment Survey
Establishment Survey
text:
Headline payrolls










Figure 1: Monthly change in total nonfarm payrolls. Use the radio to switch smoothing.

title: Establishment Survey
text:
Sectoral contributions










Figure 2: Monthly change by sector since the start of 2024.














Figure 3: Cumulative jobs added since December 2024 by sector.

title: Establishment Survey
text:
Diffusion index
The CES diffusion index is the share of industries adding jobs in a given month (with industries unchanged counted at half-weight). Readings above 50 indicate broad-based hiring; readings below 50 indicate the gains are concentrated.










Figure 4: CES diffusion index — share of tracked industries adding jobs.

title: Establishment Survey
text:
Share of private-sector gains
A Konczal-style framing: of the cumulative private-sector jobs added since December 2024, how much has gone to health care & social assistance vs the five blue-collar sectors (mining, construction, manufacturing, transport & warehousing, utilities) vs all other private industries combined? When gains are concentrated in health care, the headline NFP number is masking weakness elsewhere.










Figure 5

title: Establishment Survey
text:
Blue-collar trend gap
Each panel fits a linear trend through cumulative job changes from December 2022 to December 2024, then projects it forward. The dashed trend line is what hiring would look like at the 2023–24 pace; the solid line is actual. Negative gap = jobs below trend.










Figure 6: Cumulative jobs added since 2022Q4 in five blue-collar industries vs the 2022Q4–2024Q4 trend.

title: Establishment Survey
text:
Cyclical sensitivity tertiles
Sectors grouped (manually, by conventional economic sensitivity) into high, medium and low cyclical buckets. Rolling 3-month averages of monthly payroll change. The high-sensitivity tertile drops first in slowdowns and recovers fastest; the low-sensitivity tertile (mostly health, education and government) is much steadier.










Figure 7





Source: Haver Analytics. Joshua Bailey

title: JOLTS
text: JOLTS gives the demand side of the labour market: the stock of vacancies and flows through hires, quits, and layoffs. The key tightness reads are the vacancies-to-unemployed (V/U) ratio and the quits rate — workers quit more readily when they have outside options. Both fell back toward their pre-pandemic averages over 2024–25.

title: Jobs day overview
text:
What changed this release

Headline payrolls printed at +178 in March 2026, against a 3-month trailing average of +68. Unemployment fell from 4.4% to 4.3%.

title: Jobs day overview
text:
Headline charts










Figure 1: Total nonfarm change with smoothing toggle.














Figure 2: Civilian unemployment rate, 16+.














Figure 3: Vacancies-to-unemployed ratio.





Source: Haver Analytics. Joshua Bailey

title: Household Survey
text: Series in this section were affected by the September–November 2025 federal government shutdown: the October 2025 CPS release was missed. Those data points are linearly interpolated in the bundle so the dashboard reads continuously, but treat the imputed month with appropriate caution. See About.

title: Household Survey
text:
Headline rates










Figure 1: U-3, U-5 and U-6 — broader measures track labour-market slack beyond the headline unrate.














Figure 2: Civilian unemployment rate — toggle smoothing.

title: Household Survey
text:
Participation and employment-to-population
The 25-54 (prime-age) cuts strip out cyclical effects from the entry / exit of teenagers and retirees. Dashed lines show each series’ 2019 average as a pre-pandemic baseline.










Figure 3

title: Household Survey
text:
Sahm rule
The Sahm rule (Claudia Sahm, 2019) signals the start of a recession when the 3-month moving average of the unemployment rate rises 0.5 percentage points or more above its trailing 12-month minimum. The threshold is shown in red.










Figure 4







Table 1


Latest reading: 0.13pp — currently below the 0.5pp recession threshold.

title: Household Survey
text:
Composition of unemployment










Figure 5: Unemployment by duration. Long-duration shares lag the cycle and are watched as a slack indicator.














Figure 6: Decomposition of the unemployed pool by reason.

title: Household Survey
section: Where did the unemployment rate move?
text:
Where did the unemployment rate move?
A single-figure decomposition of the change in unrate since the December 2024 “baseline” across four demographic dimensions. Red bars = unrate up; navy = down. Comparing magnitudes across cohorts reveals whether the movement is concentrated (e.g. younger workers, less-educated, particular ethnic groups) or broad-based.










Figure 7: Change in unemployment rate since December 2024, by age, race/ethnicity, and education.














Figure 8














Figure 9














Figure 10














Figure 11: Native-born vs foreign-born unemployment, NSA so smoothed with a 6-month moving average.

title: Household Survey
text:
Labour-force flows (“bathtub”)
The unemployment level changes each month as people flow into and out of the unemployment pool. There are six gross flows; here are the four that touch the U-pool directly, plus the two flows around it.










Figure 12: Six gross labour-force flows. Monthly in grey, 3-month MA in navy.














Figure 13














Figure 14: Job-finding rate — the probability an unemployed worker finds a job within the month.





Source: Haver Analytics. Joshua Bailey
